primary_diagnosis.name: Allergic contact dermatitis (nickel/metal) with secondary excoriation

pathophysiology_summary: A type IV hypersensitivity to nickel in the belt buckle produces a sharply demarcated, geometric erythematous plaque exactly at the contact site, with intense pruritus; scratching has caused serous weeping, crusting and superficial fissuring, and an OTC antiseptic cream has added irritant contact dermatitis. Discriminators: the sharp rectangular contact-shaped plaque without an annular scaly advancing border/central clearing argues against tinea corporis; predominant pruritus with serous (not honey-crusted) weeping argues against impetigo; and the localisation entirely to an external-object contact site, sparing typical flexures, argues against an atopic dermatitis flare. Management is allergen avoidance (the definitive step), ceasing the irritant antiseptic, a potent topical corticosteroid, and itch control, with a secondary-infection safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Sharp geometric plaque matching a metal buckle with intense itch.
Annular scaly border with central clearing — not a sharp rectangle matching an object.
Honey-crusted and painless rather than intensely itchy with serous weeping.
Typically flexural and not confined to a contact-shaped patch.
Excoriated weeping skin can superinfect — screen for spread/pus/fever.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise nickel ACD from the contact-shaped plaque, stop the irritant antiseptic, and safety-net for secondary infection.

differential.evidence_basis: No spreading warmth, purulence or fever — remains a safety-net.
Geometric contact shape without an annular scaly border.
Working diagnosis: allergen avoidance, cease irritant antiseptic, potent topical steroid, itch control, infection safety-net.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection (spreading hot redness beyond the plaque, thick purulent discharge, fever)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Excoriated weeping skin can superinfect and progress to cellulitis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Missing the contact-shaped geometry and prescribing antifungals/antibiotics unnecessarily.
Not identifying/ceasing the OTC antiseptic that is worsening the dermatitis.
Prescribing a cream without the definitive step of avoiding metal-skin contact.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Patch testing to confirm the allergen
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Cease the OTC antiseptic, prescribe a potent topical corticosteroid, advise strict allergen avoidance and itch care; urgent review only if secondary infection develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Mislabelling as tinea/impetigo, or continuing the irritant antiseptic.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Allergic contact dermatitis: identify and avoid the allergen, stop irritants, apply a topical corticosteroid of appropriate potency; consider patch testing.
